concepts.title: The Constituent Assembly of India
concepts.description: The Constituent Assembly of India was formed on 9 December 1946, with 389 members representing the provinces of British India and the princes of Indian states. It drafted and adopted the Constitution of India on 26 November 1949.

concepts.title: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar's role
concepts.description: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee, which prepared the final draft of the Constitution. He played a crucial role in shaping the Constitution and is often referred to as the 'Architect of the Indian Constitution'.

concepts.title: Preamble to the Constitution
concepts.description: The Preamble to the Constitution of India is a brief introduction that sets out the objectives and ideals of the Constitution. It is a unifying document that reflects the aspirations and values of the Indian people.

concepts.title: The 22 fundamental rights
concepts.description: To remember the 22 fundamental rights, use the mnemonic 'Rights of Indians'. Each word in the sentence corresponds to the first letter of a fundamental right, in order: Right of Equality, Right of Freedom, Right of Abatement, Right of Abode, Right of Expression, Right of Association, Right of Assembly, Right of Education, Right of Employment, Right of Freedom of Religion, Right of Freedom of Speech, Right of Freedom of the Press, Right of Freedom of Trade, Right of Freedom to Move, Right of Freedom to Practice Any Profession, Right of Freedom to Residence, Right of Freedom to Security, Right of Freedom to Vote, Right of Freedom to Work, Right of Freedom to Worship, Right of Property, and Right of Protection.

concepts.title: The difference between Directive Principles and Fundamental Rights
concepts.description: Students often confuse Directive Principles and Fundamental Rights. Directive Principles are guidelines for the government, while Fundamental Rights are enforceable rights of citizens. The former is mentioned in Part IV of the Constitution, while the latter is mentioned in Part III.

concepts.title: The Constitution of India's structure
concepts.description: The Constitution of India consists of 22 parts, 395 articles, 8 schedules, and a preamble. The main parts are: Part I (The Constitution), Part II (The Government of India), Part III (Fundamental Rights), Part IV (Directive Principles), Part V (The Union and its Territory), Part VI (The States), Part VII (States in Part B of the First Schedule), Part VIII (The Union Territories), Part IX (The Panchayats), Part X (The Schedules Tribes), Part XI (The Distribution of Legislative Powers between the Union and the States), Part XII (The Finance, Property, and Revenue of the Union and the States), Part XIII (The Union and the States: Distribution of Powers), Part XIV (Services under the Union and the States), Part XV (The Elections), Part XVI (Subject-Matter of Laws), Part XVII (Constitutional Authority of Parliament to Make Laws), Part XVIII (Temporary, Transitional, and Special Provisions), Part XIX (Miscellaneous), Part XX (Amendment of the Constitution), Part XXI (Temporary Provisions Relating to the President and the Vice-President), and Part XXII (Short Title, Commencement, Legislative Enactments Continued in Force, Repeals, and Transitional and Temporary Provisions).

concepts.title: The Constitution of India came into effect
concepts.description: The Constitution of India came into effect on 26 January 1950, with Dr. Rajendra Prasad as the first President of India.

concepts.title: The role of the Indian National Congress in the framing of the Constitution
concepts.description: The Indian National Congress played a crucial role in the framing of the Constitution. The party's constitution and the Nehru Report of 1928 served as a basis for the Constitution of India.

concepts.title: The concept of the 'We, the People' of India
concepts.description: The Preamble to the Constitution begins with the phrase 'We, the People of India'. This concept signifies that the Constitution represents the collective will of the Indian people, and all citizens have a stake in its implementation.
